target.name: Agility Robotics
target.founder: Jonathan Hurst, Damion Shelton, Mikhail Jones

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series E → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.4x) to $385M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $2.0B - $2.3B.

private.name: Divergent
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series E), post-money $2.3B

data.narrative:
Agility Robotics, a Series E robotics and manufacturing company, has raised $400M+ to date. Valuation assessment draws on a peer universe of 9 private robotics firms and 1 public comparable, with primary focus on three same-sector, same/adjacent-stage peers: Divergent (Series E, $2.3B), Exotec (Series D, $2.0B), and Locus Robotics (Series F, $2.0B).

Two methods were applied. Stage-progression anchored to $385M total raised as a proxy baseline, applied a typical Series E step-up multiplier of 1.4x, yielding $539M. Peer-median triangulation across the three comparables produced a median valuation of $2.0B, with a range of $2.0B to $2.3B. The peer set reflects heterogeneous staging—one peer ahead in Series F, one behind in Series D—introducing some dispersion but maintaining sector consistency.

The triangulated analysis supports a range of $458M to $2.3B, with a median of $1.3B. The wide spread reflects tension between the conservative stage-progression method ($539M) and the peer-median approach ($2.0B), likely reflecting differences in revenue scale, customer concentration, or technology maturity relative to named peers. Key uncertainties include whether Agility's customer traction and unit economics align with the higher-valued Divergent and Locus Robotics cohort, and whether stage-appropriate financing multiples have shifted post-Series D in this sector.
